parentQuestion: Damage to the optic nerve (CN II) would result in ___.

scaffolds.q: Which cranial nerve carries visual information from your eye to your brain?
scaffolds.explain: CN II (optic nerve) carries VISUAL information from the retina to the brain. 'Optic' = vision. Without this nerve, the brain never receives the visual signals your eye detects.

wrongExplains.0: CN I (olfactory) carries SMELL information from the nose. Different sense, different nerve. Optic = vision, Olfactory = smell.
wrongExplains.2: CN III (oculomotor) MOVES the eye but doesn't carry visual information. It controls eye muscles and pupil size. CN II carries the actual image data.
wrongExplains.3: CN VIII (vestibulocochlear) carries HEARING and BALANCE from the ear. Vision uses CN II, not CN VIII.

scaffolds.q: The optic nerve connects which two structures?
scaffolds.explain: The optic nerve connects the RETINA (back of the eye) to the BRAIN (visual cortex). It's like a cable carrying video signals from a camera to a monitor.

wrongExplains.0: Ear to brain uses CN VIII (vestibulocochlear). The optic nerve is specifically for vision, not hearing.
wrongExplains.2: Nose to brain uses CN I (olfactory) for smell. The optic nerve serves the eye, not the nose.
wrongExplains.3: Tongue to brain uses CN VII and IX for taste. The optic nerve doesn't carry taste — only visual data.

scaffolds.q: If the optic nerve is completely destroyed, what happens to vision in that eye?
scaffolds.explain: Complete optic nerve destruction causes TOTAL BLINDNESS in that eye. No nerve = no signal path. Even if the eye is perfectly healthy, the brain never receives the visual information.

wrongExplains.0: Blurry vision suggests partial function — the signal is getting through but degraded. Complete destruction means NO signal at all.
wrongExplains.1: Color changes suggest partial damage to specific fibers. Complete destruction means nothing gets through — not even distorted images.
wrongExplains.3: Vision cannot improve if the nerve carrying visual signals is destroyed. No nerve = no vision, period.

scaffolds.q: CN II is classified as purely ___.
scaffolds.explain: CN II is purely SENSORY — it only carries information TO the brain (visual signals). It doesn't control any muscles. The three purely sensory cranial nerves are CN I, II, and VIII.

wrongExplains.0: Motor nerves carry commands FROM the brain to muscles. CN II doesn't control muscles — it carries visual information TO the brain.
wrongExplains.2: Mixed nerves have both sensory and motor fibers. CN II only has sensory fibers carrying visual input.
wrongExplains.3: Autonomic nerves control involuntary functions like heart rate. CN II carries conscious visual perception.

scaffolds.q: Each eye has its own optic nerve. If the RIGHT optic nerve is damaged, which eye loses vision?
scaffolds.explain: RIGHT optic nerve damage = RIGHT eye blindness. Each eye has its own dedicated optic nerve. Damage to one doesn't affect the other eye's nerve.

wrongExplains.0: The left optic nerve serves the left eye. Right nerve damage affects the right eye only.
wrongExplains.2: Each optic nerve is independent until they partially cross at the optic chiasm. Before the chiasm, one nerve = one eye.
wrongExplains.3: The damaged nerve definitely can't carry signals. The right eye will lose vision if its optic nerve is destroyed.

scaffolds.q: Where does the optic nerve send visual information?
scaffolds.explain: The optic nerve sends signals to the VISUAL CORTEX in the occipital lobe (back of the brain). This is where your brain actually 'sees' and interprets visual information.

wrongExplains.0: The auditory cortex processes SOUND from CN VIII. Visual information goes to a different brain area entirely.
wrongExplains.2: The motor cortex controls MOVEMENT. Visual processing happens in the visual cortex. Different functions, different locations.
wrongExplains.3: The spinal cord handles body sensation and movement. Cranial nerve signals go directly to the brain, not the spinal cord.

scaffolds.q: The eye itself is healthy, but the optic nerve is severed. Can this person see from that eye?
options: No, signals can't reach the brain
scaffolds.explain: NO vision is possible. The eye may detect light perfectly, but without the optic nerve, that information NEVER REACHES the brain. Vision requires both detection (eye) AND perception (brain).

wrongExplains.0: The eye 'works' in the sense that photoreceptors still respond to light, but you can't SEE without the brain receiving those signals.
wrongExplains.2: No visual information of any kind — color, shape, movement, nothing — can reach the brain without the optic nerve.
wrongExplains.3: Light level doesn't matter if the nerve is cut. No signal path = no vision under any conditions.

scaffolds.q: What's the difference between CN II (optic) and CN III (oculomotor)?
options: CN II carries vision TO brain; CN III moves the eye
CN II moves the eye; CN III carries vision
scaffolds.explain: CN II is SENSORY (carries visual signals TO the brain). CN III is mostly MOTOR (moves the eye, controls pupil). Different jobs: II = seeing, III = moving.

wrongExplains.1: This is backwards. CN II carries visual information (sensory). CN III controls eye muscles (motor).
wrongExplains.2: Only CN II carries vision. CN III controls eye MOVEMENT and pupil size — it doesn't transmit visual images.
wrongExplains.3: Only CN III moves the eye (along with CN IV and VI). CN II is purely sensory — it doesn't control any muscles.

scaffolds.q: A patient has a brain tumor pressing on the right optic nerve. What symptom would you expect?
options: Vision loss in the right eye
scaffolds.explain: Pressure on the RIGHT optic nerve causes RIGHT eye vision loss. The nerve can't transmit signals when compressed. This is a classic sign of tumors near the optic pathway.

wrongExplains.0: Deafness would involve CN VIII (vestibulocochlear), not the optic nerve. Different nerve, different location.
wrongExplains.2: Arm weakness involves motor pathways and spinal nerves, not cranial nerve II. The optic nerve only handles vision.
wrongExplains.3: Loss of smell involves CN I (olfactory). Optic nerve compression affects vision, not smell.
